title: Medical Card — How to Apply and Eligibility — HSE
content:
A Medical Card entitles you to a range of health services free of charge, including GP visits, prescribed medications, in-patient and out-patient hospital services, dental, ophthalmic, and aural services. The Medical Card is means-tested — your income must fall below certain guidelines.

What is covered by a Medical Card
- GP visits (free, including out-of-hours GP)
- Prescribed medicines and drugs (from the Medical Card formulary)
- All in-patient public hospital services
- Out-patient public hospital services
- Dental treatment by HSE-contracted dentists
- Eye tests and a contribution toward glasses
- Hearing aids and repairs
- Maternity and infant care

Who is automatically eligible
The following receive Medical Cards without a means test:
- Everyone aged 70 and over (special over-70s assessment applies)
- Children in the care of the State (foster care, etc.)
- EU visitors under the EU Cross-Border Directive
- People receiving certain DSP payments (Domiciliary Care Allowance in some cases)

Income guidelines (weekly gross income, 2025)
For people under 70:
- Single person, no dependants, aged 23-65: €184 per week
- Single person with 1-2 dependants under 16: €201 per week
- Single person with 3+ dependants under 16: €218 per week
- Married couple, no dependants: €268 per week
- Married couple with 1-2 dependants under 16: €285 per week
- Married couple with 3+ dependants under 16: €302 per week

For people over 70:
- Single person: €550 per week (more generous assessment)
- Married couple: €1,050 per week combined

Income is assessed after deductions for income tax, PRSI, USC, work-related expenses, and childcare costs for children under 16.

How to apply
Apply online at MyWelfare.ie using your MyGovID or PPSN. Alternatively, complete form MC1 available from your GP's surgery, your local health centre, or hse.ie. Attach supporting documents including proof of income (payslips, social welfare statement), proof of address, and your PPSN.

Processing times
Routine applications take 4-8 weeks. Emergency Medical Cards (for people in urgent medical need) can be issued within 24 hours — ask your GP or consultant to confirm urgency.

Medical Card review
Medical Cards are reviewed periodically. You will be asked to renew and confirm your income details remain within guidelines. If your income increases above the guidelines, your Medical Card may be withdrawn.

title: GP Visit Card — HSE
content:
A GP Visit Card entitles you to free GP visits but does not cover the cost of prescriptions, hospital visits, or other health services (unlike a full Medical Card). It is designed for people who earn slightly above the Medical Card income limits.

Who is automatically eligible for a GP Visit Card
The following people receive a GP Visit Card without any means test:
- Children under 8
- All people aged 70 and over
- People with a medical card also have GP access through that card
- People receiving Carer's Allowance automatically qualify

Means-tested GP Visit Card
If you do not qualify automatically, you can apply for a GP Visit Card if your income is above Medical Card limits but below the higher GP Visit Card income limits.

Income guidelines for means-tested GP Visit Card (2025, weekly gross)
- Single person, no dependants, aged 23-65: €304 per week
- Married couple, no dependants: €441 per week
- These limits are approximately 50-60% higher than Medical Card limits

What a GP Visit Card covers
- Unlimited free GP consultations (in-hours and out-of-hours)
- It does NOT cover prescriptions (you still pay for medicines)
- It does NOT cover hospital visits, dental, or optical services

Drug Payment Scheme
If you have a GP Visit Card but need prescription medicines regularly, you should also register for the Drug Payment Scheme, which caps monthly prescription costs.

How to apply
Apply online through MyWelfare.ie or complete form GPC1 from your GP's surgery or local health centre. Include proof of identity, address, and income.

Over 70 automatic registration
People aged 70 and over are automatically registered for the GP Visit Card. You do not need to apply — the HSE will contact you when you reach 70.

Children under 8
All children under 8 are entitled to free GP visits regardless of family income. To register, contact your GP and ask them to register your child's PPSN with the HSE. If your child does not have a PPSN, apply through your local Intreo office.

title: Drug Payment Scheme (DPS) — HSE
content:
The Drug Payment Scheme (DPS) limits how much any individual or family pays for approved prescribed drugs and medicines in a single calendar month. It is for people who do not have a Medical Card.

Monthly cap
Under the Drug Payment Scheme, the maximum you pay for prescribed medicines per calendar month is €80 per family or individual. Any costs above €80 are covered by the HSE. The cap was reduced to €80 in April 2024 (previously €100).

Who can register
Anyone who is ordinarily resident in Ireland and does not have a Medical Card can register for the DPS. There is no means test and no income limit.

What is covered
The DPS covers:
- Prescription medicines that are approved for supply under the scheme
- Certain medicines that are specifically prescribed for certain medical conditions
- Appliances and bandages on prescription

What is NOT covered
- Medicines that are not on the approved DPS list
- Over-the-counter medicines
- Medicines supplied in hospital (inpatient)

How to register
Registration is free and is done at any pharmacy in Ireland. Bring your PPSN and proof of identity. Once registered, present your DPS card at the pharmacy each time you collect a prescription. Your pharmacist will track your spending toward the monthly cap.

Family registration
You can register as a family unit on the DPS — a family consists of you, your spouse/civil partner, and children under 18 (or under 23 if in full-time education). A family pays a maximum of €80 per month combined, regardless of how many family members are on the scheme.

Refunds
If you paid for prescriptions before registering for the DPS, you can claim a refund for the current year only. Contact the HSE Local Health Office with your receipts.

DPS and other schemes
The DPS works alongside other HSE schemes. If you are on the Long-Term Illness Scheme (LTI), your LTI medicines are free and do not count toward your DPS monthly cap.

title: Long-Term Illness Scheme (LTI) — HSE
content:
The Long-Term Illness (LTI) Scheme provides free medicines and medical and surgical appliances for the treatment of specified conditions. If you have one of the listed conditions and are registered for the scheme, you do not pay for prescribed medicines related to that condition.

Conditions covered
The following conditions are covered by the LTI Scheme:
- Acute Leukaemia
- Cerebral Palsy
- Cystic Fibrosis
- Diabetes insipidus
- Diabetes mellitus (Type 1 and Type 2)
- Epilepsy
- Haemophilia
- Hydrocephalus
- Mental illness (serious — requiring long-term treatment)
- Multiple Sclerosis
- Muscular Dystrophies
- Parkinsonism
- Phenylketonuria
- Spina Bifida
- Motor Neurone Disease
- Conditions arising from use of Thalidomide

What is free under the LTI
Medicines, drugs, and medical appliances that are prescribed specifically for your listed condition are free. This includes insulin, anti-epileptic drugs, MS disease-modifying therapies, and other condition-specific treatments.

General medicines
Medicines for conditions other than your listed LTI condition are not covered under the scheme — you pay for those through the Drug Payment Scheme or directly.

No means test
There is no means test and no income limit for the LTI Scheme. Eligibility is based solely on the medical diagnosis.

How to register
Contact your GP or hospital consultant who treats your condition. They will complete a registration form on your behalf. Once registered, you receive an LTI card. Show your LTI card at the pharmacy when collecting prescriptions related to your condition.

Changing pharmacy
You can use any LHO-registered pharmacy in Ireland with your LTI card. You are not restricted to one pharmacy.

Related schemes
- Medical Card: provides broader cover across all health services
- Drug Payment Scheme: caps monthly prescription costs for people without a Medical Card
- High-Tech Medicines Scheme: covers certain expensive specialised medicines (e.g., for Hepatitis C, cancer, rheumatoid arthritis) for eligible patients

title: Nursing Home Support Scheme — Fair Deal — HSE
content:
The Nursing Home Support Scheme, known as the Fair Deal, is a State-funded scheme that helps to meet the cost of long-term nursing home care. Under the scheme, you pay a contribution toward the cost of your care based on your income and assets, and the State pays the remainder.

How your contribution is calculated
Your contribution is assessed as follows:
- 80% of your assessable income per week (income from all sources)
- Plus 7.5% of the value of your assessable assets per year

Assets include savings, investments, and property — except your principal private residence (your home) for the first 3 years. After 3 years, 7.5% per year of your home's value is also included (effectively up to 22.5% over 3 years maximum — this is the 'Care and Support' loan, repaid on the sale or transfer of the property).

The State pays the difference between your contribution and the cost of care.

Eligibility
Anyone aged 18 or over who needs long-term nursing home care can apply. There is no upper age limit. You can apply to any nursing home registered with HIQA (Health Information and Quality Authority) that participates in the Fair Deal scheme.

The application process
Step 1: Apply to the HSE for a financial assessment. Complete the Nursing Home Support Scheme application and provide financial details (income, assets, PPSN).
Step 2: HSE completes the financial assessment and issues your weekly contribution figure.
Step 3: Apply to a nursing home that participates in the scheme. The HSE will commit funding once you have a nursing home place.

Nursing home options
You can choose any registered private, voluntary, or public nursing home that participates in the Fair Deal scheme. Different nursing homes have different charges — the State pays the nursing home at the approved national price for your care category; if the nursing home charges more, you must pay the difference.

Protecting your home
Your principal private residence is protected for the first 3 years. Even if you sell your home, the maximum you will have paid toward care from the home's value is 22.5% (7.5% per year for 3 years). This is structured as a loan that is repaid only when the home is sold or transferred.

Care for a Couple
If both members of a couple need nursing home care, each is assessed separately. If one stays at home while the other enters a nursing home, the asset assessment is reduced — only 50% of the couple's shared assets is assessed against the person in care.

How to apply
Download the application form from the HSE website or contact the Fair Deal unit at your Local Health Office. The HSE aims to complete financial assessments within 6 weeks.
